name: Von Restorff (Isolation) Effect: Emphasise the One That Matters
trigger: A screen needs to draw attention to a single most-important element among peers, such as a primary CTA, a recommended plan, a critical alert, or a key metric.
recommendation: Make the single most important item visually distinct from its surroundings so it stands out and is remembered: give the primary action a unique, higher-emphasis treatment, distinguish the recommended option, and reserve a strong accent for the one thing you most want noticed. The effect depends on scarcity, so emphasise sparingly.
rejection: Reject giving several elements equal high emphasis (which cancels the effect and creates competition), styling every button as primary, and over-using accent colour so nothing stands out.
validation: Confirm there is exactly one clear primary emphasis per view or decision
Check competing high-emphasis elements are demoted to secondary or tertiary
Run a five-second test to verify users notice the intended element first
evidence: Von Restorff (1933): an item that differs from its surroundings is more likely to be remembered
Visual hierarchy and pre-attentive processing research support a single dominant focal point
